data.title: GameStop piles up more cash in Q3 even as sales fall off
data.description:
GameStop Corp. (NYSE:GME) edged higher in post-market trading on Tuesday after disclosing its third quarter earnings report.

Revenue fell 20.4% year-over-year for the quarter that ended on November 2 to $860.3 million. Hardware and accessories sales accounted for 48.5% of total sales vs. 53.7% a year ago, while software sales rose to 31.6% of the mix from 29.8% last year. Collectibles revenue was up to 19.9% of the total vs. 16.5% a year ago.

The Texas-based company said selling, general and administrative expenses were 32.8% of sales, compared to 27.5% of sales a year ago. Cost of sales fell to 70.1% of total sales, vs. 73.9% a year ago. GameStop (GME) recorded net income of $17.4 million in Q3, vs. a net income loss of $3.1 a year ago. EPS was $0.06 for the quarter, vs. -$0.01 a year ago.

On the balance sheet, GameStop (GME) ended the quarter with $4.62 billion in cash and merchandise inventory was reported at $830.2 million vs. $1.02 billion a year ago. GameStop (GME) had long-term debt of $9.6 million at the end of the quarter. The company said it does not anticipate any further at-the-market offerings and sale of its common stock during the current fiscal year.

GameStop (GME) did not release any comments from CEO Ryan Cohen, and the retailer will not be holding an earnings conference call.

Wedbush Securities is one of just a handful of firms with coverage on GameStop (GME). The firm has an Underperform rating on the retailer and a 12-month price target of $10. Analyst Michael Pachter warned that GameStop (GME) faces "insurmountable" barriers and has no clear strategy to deploy capital to justify the trading premium over its cash value.

Shares of GameStop (GME) showed an initial gain of 3.3% in postmarket trading. Short interest on GME stands at 7.1% of the total float. The market cap of GameStop (GME) is ~$12.5 billion.

data.title: Lionsgate Studios gains as activist Anson Funds reveals stake, may push for sale
data.description:
Lionsgate Studios (NASDAQ:LION) rose 1% after activist investor Anson Funds disclosed it had accumulated a stake in the movie and television studio.

Anson argued that the studio behind "The Hunger Games" and "John Wick" is undervalued and should evaluate its options, including a sale, Sagar Gupta, the firm's head of activism, said at the Bloomberg Activism Forum in New York City on Tuesday. He believes LION has "well over" 100% upside.

Gupta believes the studio would attract takeover interest, or the company could also look at selling its unscripted television and 3 Arts talent management businesses.

data.title: Marvell announces breakthrough in custom high-bandwidth memory
data.description:
• Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) made several announcements late on Wednesday, including a break through in custom high-bandwidth memory that's used in artificial intelligence processors.
  • Marvell said its new AI accelerator architecture, or XPU, will allow for 25% more compute power and 33% greater memory, while also allowing for improved power efficiency. The company is working with Micron (MU), Samsung and SK hynix on custom HBM processors for the accelerators.
  • "The leading cloud data center operators have scaled with custom infrastructure," said Marvell executive Will Chu in a statement. "Enhancing XPUs by tailoring HBM for specific performance, power, and total cost of ownership is the latest step in a new paradigm in the way AI accelerators are designed and delivered. We're very grateful to work with leading memory designers to accelerate this revolution and, help cloud data center operators continue to scale their XPUs and infrastructure for the AI era."
  • The company also made a pair of related announcements, introducing a 1.6 Tbps LPO chipset to be used in optical networking to increase the speed and reach associated with copper cables; and a Coherent-lite 1.6 Tbps O-band-optimized chipset for data centers.
  • Marvell shares rose 1% in extended trading on the back of the announcements.

data.title: Quantum stocks accelerate on Google's Willow chip
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks surged Tuesday following Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) announcement on Monday of Willow, a potentially revolutionary quantum chip.

Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) led the pack skyward as it rocketed 45%, while Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT) edged up 1.8% and D-Wave Quantum (NYSE:QBTS) increased 0.4%. Google climbed 5% higher to close the day at $186. Google is closing in on its all-time high of $192 that it reached on July 10, 2024.

James Foord, Investing Group Leader for The Pragmatic Investor, said in an analysis today that Willow could revolutionize computing and lead to breakthroughs in artificial intelligence, health care and other disciplines.

"Willow's advancements in quantum error correction and processing power are significant, completing tasks in minutes that would take classical computers septillions of years," he said.

"The largest beneficiary of Quantum computing will be, undoubtedly, healthcare and specifically drug discovery," Foord noted. "This is already being revolutionized by AI, and paired with QC, this field will be completely revolutionized. Quantum Computers would be able to run millions of incredibly accurate simulations on various new drugs and treatments, leading to exponential advancement in the field."

data.title: Apollomics regains compliance with Nasdaq’s minimum bid price requirement
data.description:
• Apollomics (NASDAQ:APLM) on Tuesday said it has received a notification from Nasdaq, stating that the company has regained compliance with the requirement to maintain a minimum closing bid price of $1.00 per share, and Nasdaq has determined to continue the listing of its Class A ordinary shares on the Nasdaq Capital Market.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: US Steel plunges after report Biden set to block $14B sale to Nippon Steel
data.description:
US Steel (NYSE:X) sunk 14% after a report that President Joe Biden will formally block the $14 billion sale to Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) over national security concerns.

Biden will block the deal once the Committee on Foreign Investment in the U.S. decides on it either by Dec. 22 or Dec. 23, according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. It's not known what CFIUS will decide.

Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) and US steel are likely to pursue litigation if Biden moves with a block of the transaction, according to the report.

Developing story ...

data.title: Albertsons falls after report federal judge blocks $24.6B sale to Kroger
data.description:
Albertsons (NYSE:ACI) dropped as much as 10% after a federal judge in Oregon blocked Kroger's (KR) $25 planned purchased. Kroger rose 4.7%.

U.S. District Judge Adrienne Nelson found in favor of the US Federal Trade Commission, according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited the judge's decision.

The federal trial in Portland, Oregon ended in September and investors were waiting for the judge to opine on the deal.

The FTC sued to block the mega supermarket combination in February, saying the supermarket deal would lead to higher prices for consumers. Eight states and Washington, D.C. also teamed up with the regulator to halt the deal.

Albertsons (ACI) said at the start of the federal trial that if the FTC succeeds in blocking the deal, the chain will likely have to cut jobs, close stores, exit certain markets or ultimately sell the chain.

Developing story ...

data.title: Will November's CPI and PPI map out a more difficult path to 2% inflation?
data.description:
Two important gauges of inflation will be released this week ahead of the Federal Open Market Committee's Dec. 18 rate decision. The November Consumer Price Index will come out on Wednesday and the November Producer Price Index will be issued on Thursday.

The two measures will sketch out some of the U.S. inflation picture, offering different perspectives of the economy — the CPI from the household view and the PPI from businesses' view.

The CPI is expected to show a 0.3% M/M increase, accelerating from 0.2% in October. Excluding food and energy prices, which jump around month-to-month, the CPI is expected to rise 0.3% M/M, unchanged from the previous month's increase.

The Cleveland Fed's inflation nowcasting expects November to show a 0.26% increase in CPI and 0.27% rise in core CPI.

Meanwhile, the November headline PPI is projected to accelerate by 0.3% M/M from 0.2% in October. The core PPI, excluding food and energy, is seen increasing 0.2%, slowing from 0.3% in the prior month.

The relationship between CPI and PPI "really holds up when prices are increasing," said Daniel Doderer, economist and research director at Flack Global Metals, a steel and aluminum supplier. "If PPI is going higher, we view it as a pretty significant risk of CPI going higher, but when PPI is going lower — the producer side of prices —it doesn't necessarily translate to consumers getting relief."

Flack Global Metals has seen inflation on the PPI side "kind of bubbling up," while CPI has been "hovering lower," Doderer said. "Even though we're seeing continued disinflation, I think it's disappointing a lot of forecasts."

The economist generally agrees with consensus for both CPI and PPI.

"A hot inflation report would undermine the rally of high-flying magnificent seven stocks," said Chris Lau, Investing Group Leader of DIY Value Investing.

Inflation expectations are high in the food sector, while housing inflation remains stubbornly "sticky." On the flip side, energy prices are falling, Lau points out.

SA Analyst Damir Tokic adds that consumers are getting squeezed by inflation in restaurants, hospitals, electricity bills, rents, and car insurance.

Looking ahead, Flack Global Metals' Doderer sees potential for inflation to perk up. For example, the small business confidence rose to its highest level in about three and a half years. That could lead to higher prices as demand for goods increases, he said.

He sees a risk of "sticky and potentially higher inflation." Increased tariffs and dramatically reduced immigration could increase prices, too. The biggest risk is that a wage price spiral develops due to immigration policy heating up the labor market, he said.

Still, Doderer's base case is that the labor market continue to cool "from a very healthy place, but if the industrial side heats up too fast, that could keep 2% (inflation) out of reach at least for the next year."

The lack of forward guidance by the Federal Reserve complicates the picture, making it more challenging for businesses to map out plans.

And while the CPI and PPI numbers will give the Fed's policymaking arm an idea of how inflation fared in November, the Commerce Department's personal consumption expenditure price index will give it an even better sense of whether inflation is heading toward's the central bank's 2% inflation goal. That, however, will have to wait until the Fed's Jan. 28-29 meeting because the PCE won't be released until two days after the December meeting. By Jan. 29, the Fed will have another CPI and PPI report and another employment report.

Flack Global Metals' Doderer points to a "kind of whipsaw that can go into long-term planning when there's really a lack of clarity on where (monetary) policy is headed. That's really been what's standing in the way more than the actual level of rates."
